title: Hand-stitching a torn upholstery seam
subtitle: Repair a split seam on a sofa cushion or chair arm with a curved needle and waxed thread
excerpt: How to mend a torn seam on a sofa cushion, chair arm, or piped edge by hand. Covers the slip-stitch and the ladder stitch, the right needle and thread, knotting the start and finish, and the small bridging step before any tear gets bigger.

glossaryTerms.definition: An invisible hand stitch worked from the right side of a seam. The thread crosses the gap between the two folded fabric edges and the stitches sit hidden inside the fold.
A heavy linen or polyester thread coated with beeswax. The wax stops the thread fraying and holds the knot firm. Sold on a card or a spool in the upholstery section of any haberdashery.

content.text: A torn seam shows the cushion lining or the foam through the gap. The longer the tear is left, the wider it gets. A
ladder stitch
in
waxed upholstery thread
closes a 50 mm or 75 mm tear in 10 minutes and is almost invisible from the right side.
Step 1, inspect the tear

Open the seam gently with the fingers to check the original stitching has come apart cleanly. A clean break of the original thread is straightforward to re-stitch. A fabric tear where the cloth itself has split needs a patch behind the tear before the seam can be closed; a stitch through torn cloth will pull through again.
Step 2, thread the needle and knot the end

Cut a 600 mm length of waxed thread. Thread one end through the eye of a curved upholstery needle and pull through to half the length, so the stitch runs as a single thread. Tie a small overhand knot at the long end.
Step 3, set the first stitch

Push the needle into the fabric 10 mm before the start of the tear, from the inside of the seam. Bring the needle out through the seam fold. The knot is now hidden inside the seam. Pull the thread snug.
Step 4, work the ladder stitch

Push the needle into the fabric on the opposite side of the tear, directly across from the exit point. Run the needle inside the fold for 3 mm and bring it out. The thread now crosses the gap horizontally. Cross back to the first side, run inside the fold for 3 mm, and bring out. Continue along the tear, crossing the gap with each stitch. After every five stitches, pull the thread gently to draw the two fabric edges together. The stitches sit inside the fold and the seam closes invisibly.
Step 5, finish 10 mm past the tear

Carry the stitching 10 mm past the end of the tear into sound fabric. Tie off with three half-hitches in a row, each pulled snug against the seam. Push the needle through the seam into the cushion or arm core, bring out 50 mm away, and pull the thread taut. Cut the thread close to the fabric; the cut end disappears into the cushion.
Step 6, dress the seam

Run a fingertip along the closed seam. The two folded edges should sit flat against each other with no puckering. A small puff at one point means a stitch is too tight; ease it with a gentle pull on the surrounding fabric. The repair is now ready for normal use.
Variations

On a piped seam (a seam with a corded edge), pick the ladder stitch up through the piping rather than across the gap. The piping hides the stitch from view. On a leather seam, switch the curved needle for a leather saddle-stitch needle pair and use waxed leather thread; the ladder stitch does not work on leather because the thread shows on the surface.
Care

A repaired seam holds for years if the original cushion is sound. Re-check the seam every six months for the first year; a re-tear at one end means the original thread was old and the rest of the seam may go too. A full re-stitch of the whole seam is the right call once two repairs are in.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Stitches show as visible dots on the right side
attrs.cause: The needle was pushed too deep into the fabric, picking up surface fibres rather than running inside the fold.
attrs.fix: Pull the visible stitches out with a seam ripper. Re-stitch, keeping the needle inside the fold for the 3 mm run between exits.

attrs.symptom: The seam puckers where it has been re-stitched
attrs.cause: The thread was pulled too tight at each stitch, gathering the fabric.
attrs.fix: Cut the new stitches. Re-stitch with light tension. Draw the stitches up only every five stitches, just enough to bring the edges together.

attrs.symptom: The seam tears again at the same point a month later
attrs.cause: The fabric is fatigued, or the cushion is over-stuffed and putting steady pressure on the seam.
attrs.fix: Open the seam, fit a strip of upholstery webbing or canvas as a patch on the inside, and re-stitch through both layers.
